does: This paper introduces DECOR, a multi-agent framework for detecting and auditing deception in large language model outputs. The core method decomposes input contexts into atomic informational units and evaluates each unit across four dimensions of manipulation derived from Information Manipulation Theory, generating fine-grained manipulation profiles for individual facts before aggregating them into a global deception index. DECOR achieves state-of-the-art performance on established single-turn and multi-turn deception detection benchmarks across real-world domains, generalizes across 15 frontier LLM models, and outperforms competitive black-box baselines while providing interpretable outputs showing exactly which facts were distorted and through which manipulation mechanisms.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about subtle deceptive behaviors in LLMs that existing coarse-grained detection methods fail to capture. It extends prior research on LLM trustworthiness and honesty by moving beyond binary deception judgments toward granular mechanistic understanding of how models manipulate information. The paper responds to limitations in black-box auditing approaches by grounding its method in Information Manipulation Theory, a framework from communication and rhetoric studies that systematizes deceptive techniques like omission, focus-shifting, and obfuscation. DECOR synthesizes multi-agent reasoning patterns with fine-grained fact-level analysis, building on recent advances in using multiple LLM perspectives for improved robustness and interpretability in evaluation tasks.
enables: This framework enables future work on interpretable deception detection by providing a systematic taxonomy and measurement apparatus for understanding how LLMs manipulate information. The fact-level manipulation profiles could support development of targeted interventions for reducing specific deceptive behaviors in model training and deployment. The approach opens pathways for studying how different model architectures and training procedures produce different deception signatures, potentially informing constitutional AI methods and alignment techniques. DECOR's generalization across multiple frontier models suggests the framework could become a standard auditing tool for responsible LLM deployment, enabling security teams to identify and monitor emerging deceptive capabilities as models scale, and supporting research into whether deception represents fundamental conflicts between model capabilities and human-specified objectives.
